name: Science Education 11–12: Plan, Assess and Report
week0Message: Have a look around at the first few tiles below and finish up the Week 0 section — we'll get started next week!

learningOutcomes.label: Lesson Sequence Design
learningOutcomes.description: Plan and present a lesson sequence that incorporates appropriate pedagogy, exhibits constructivist ideas about teaching and learning, reflects knowledge of students' different approaches to learning, acknowledges Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives, and addresses identified specific outcomes and provides a meaningful evaluation strategy.

learningOutcomes.label: Resources, ICT & Safe Approaches
learningOutcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of a range of strategies, resources and ICT to support student learning, including safe approaches, and responsiveness to diverse linguistic, cultural, religious and socioeconomic backgrounds.

learningOutcomes.label: Activities, Assessment & Programming
learningOutcomes.description: Select a range of suitable activities, design assessment instruments and incorporate syllabus outcomes in programming for the purpose of facilitating student learning and for the purpose of assessing student achievement.

learningOutcomes.label: Feedback & Reflective Practice
learningOutcomes.description: Plan timely and purposeful oral and written feedback to students and demonstrate an ability to reflect on and evaluate their own teaching and modify their practice accordingly.

assessmentTasks.title: Teaching and Learning Program — Model Construction Unit
assessmentTasks.rationale: The term 'model' appears in every science discipline syllabus document. Using models to explain phenomena is a fundamental principle of science.
assessmentTasks.aim: Plan and teach a unit culminating in students constructing a physical model to demonstrate their understanding. Demonstrate application of discipline content, syllabus knowledge, and pedagogical principles for a given cohort.

parts.title: Task outline and forum post
parts.description: Complete missing sections of the supplied assessment-as-learning task outline and post a 100-word summary to the dedicated forum by 8 March.
requirements: Complete highlighted sections of the provided task outline using your chosen NSW science discipline syllabus
Choose individual or small group model construction task — justify this in Part C
Post 100-word forum summary naming: science discipline, topic, Module/Unit/s, group or individual assessment, and any major first-hand experience

parts.title: Unit program
parts.description: Five-week teaching and learning program for a given cohort, culminating in student model construction.
requirements: Five weeks, one A4 page per week on the supplied template
Integrate 10 hours of model construction and portfolio time across the five weeks as you see fit
Each week: high-level teacher/student activity detail, suggested learning activities and resources, differentiation for the named cohort, at least two formative assessment tasks
Address given Skills outcomes and the single Knowledge and Understanding outcome defined in Part A
Must be based on NSW syllabus documents
parts.note: Word equivalent: research, critical thinking, and curation effort is recognised — actual word count may be less than 1500.

parts.title: Justification essay
parts.description: Short essay justifying pedagogical decisions made throughout the unit planning process.
requirements: Explain why key pedagogical decisions were made
Cite literature from supplied materials and wider reading
Demonstrate how all learners in the given cohort are supported, using examples from your unit program

parts.title: Feedback planning
parts.description: Clearly articulated feedback plan embedded within the unit program for the model construction task.
requirements: Specify when feedback will be provided relative to student work submission
State the form feedback will take (oral, written, peer, self-assessment)
Explain how feedback language will be differentiated for Aboriginal students and EAL/D learners
Explain how feedback will inform subsequent teaching decisions
Connect feedback planning explicitly to the syllabus outcomes addressed

parts.title: Personal reflection
parts.description: Brief reflection on learning throughout the assessment task.
requirements: What worked well, what would you do differently?
When were you most engaged and why?

rubric.criterion: Task outline completion and forum post

HD.descriptor: Task outline accurate, complete, and professionally presented. Concept, year level, and outcomes correctly identified. Clear alignment between model, syllabus outcomes, and assessment purpose. Forum post concise, coherent, and clearly communicates intent.

D.descriptor: Task outline accurate and complete. Concept and outcomes correctly identified with clear alignment. Forum post clearly communicates the planned assessment.

C.descriptor: Task outline mostly accurate with minor errors or omissions. Concept and outcomes identified but alignment may be brief or descriptive. Forum post addresses requirements but lacks some clarity or detail.

P.descriptor: Some inaccuracies or missing elements. Alignment between model and outcomes unclear or underdeveloped. Forum post partially addresses task requirements.

N.descriptor: Major inaccuracies or missing components. Outcomes not correctly identified or misaligned. Forum post not submitted or does not address requirements.

rubric.criterion: Unit structure, constructivist pedagogy, differentiation, NSW syllabus alignment

HD.descriptor: Coherent 5-week NSW Stage 6 unit. Logical progression toward model construction. Constructivist pedagogy embedded throughout. Differentiation specific and responsive to named cohort (incl. Aboriginal and EAL/D learners). Outcomes and content explicitly and accurately integrated.

D.descriptor: Clear 5-week NSW-aligned unit with logical progression. Constructivist pedagogy evident. Differentiation addresses cohort with specific strategies. Outcomes/content accurate and clearly identified.

C.descriptor: NSW-aligned structure present. Progression generally clear. Constructivist elements evident. Differentiation appropriate but may lack depth. Outcomes mostly accurate. Some acknowledgement of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander students and EAL/D learners, though depth or specificity may vary.

P.descriptor: Unit present but loosely aligned to NSW syllabus. Constructivist pedagogy inconsistent. Differentiation general. Outcome links incomplete or unclear. Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives acknowledged but not specifically integrated into planned activities.

N.descriptor: No coherent structure. Not aligned to NSW syllabus. Constructivist pedagogy absent. Differentiation absent. Outcomes missing or inaccurate. No acknowledgement of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander students or EAL/D learners.

HD.descriptor: Resources intentionally selected to deepen conceptual learning across the unit with clear rationale for how each supports specific learning goals. Range of resource types evident. ICT purposeful and enhances inquiry, collaboration, or modelling. Resources appropriate, engaging and safe. Safety considerations explicitly addressed with reference to relevant guidelines (e.g., CSIS).

D.descriptor: Resources clearly support learning goals and model task. ICT enhances learning meaningfully. Resources and approaches generally appropriate and safe.

C.descriptor: Resources relevant and appropriate. ICT contributes to learning. Safety considerations mentioned where relevant.

P.descriptor: Resources adequate but generic or loosely connected. ICT minimal or substitutive. Safety considerations present but may be generic or incomplete.

N.descriptor: Resources inappropriate, irrelevant, or absent. ICT absent or misused. Safety considerations absent or inappropriate.

rubric.criterion: Learning activities aligned to syllabus outcomes

HD.descriptor: Activities explicitly aligned to NSW outcomes and scaffold toward model construction. Clear constructivist strategies evident. Descriptions detailed and implementable. Range of activity types evident across the unit (investigations, discussions, modelling, analysis, collaborative tasks). No single activity mode dominates.

D.descriptor: Activities aligned and build toward model task. Constructivist approach evident. Descriptions clear.

C.descriptor: Activities aligned and appropriate. Some constructivist elements. Descriptions generally clear.

P.descriptor: Activities partially aligned. Limited constructivist pedagogy. Descriptions lack clarity.

HD.descriptor: Two or more purposeful formative tasks embedded. Feedback specific and actionable. Clear explanation of how evidence informs future teaching and supports model completion. Coherent evaluation strategy explicitly connecting formative and summative tasks to targeted syllabus outcomes.

D.descriptor: Two formative tasks embedded. Feedback linked to learning. Teaching adjustments explained.

C.descriptor: Formative tasks present and appropriate. Feedback mentioned. Some explanation of instructional response.

P.descriptor: Limited formative assessment. Feedback minimal. Little explanation of instructional adjustment.

N.descriptor: Formative assessment missing or unclear. No meaningful feedback or instructional response.

HD.descriptor: Coherent, well-structured argument clearly explaining why key pedagogical decisions were made. Explicitly links decisions to cohort needs, syllabus outcomes, and constructivist principles. Uses integrated and synthesised peer-reviewed literature.

D.descriptor: Clear and well-developed justification. Links to cohort and syllabus explicit. Literature well integrated and supports reasoning.

C.descriptor: Sound justification. Links to cohort and syllabus present but more descriptive than analytical. Literature supports claims.

P.descriptor: Basic justification. Links to cohort or syllabus limited or general. Minimal peer-reviewed literature.

N.descriptor: Justification missing, unclear, or largely descriptive. Little or no relevant literature.

rubric.criterion: Justification of resources curated for the unit

HD.descriptor: Clear selection criteria. Explicitly explains why chosen resources are superior for this cohort and unit. Links resource choice to outcomes, differentiation, and ICT integration. Uses literature to support evaluative reasoning.

D.descriptor: Clearly explains why selected resources are appropriate and effective. Links to cohort and learning goals evident. Literature supports justification.

C.descriptor: Adequate explanation of why resources were selected. Some links to cohort or learning goals. Literature used but may be descriptive.

P.descriptor: Limited explanation of why resources were chosen. Weak or general links to cohort or outcomes. Minimal literature support.

N.descriptor: Resources not justified or inappropriate. No clear reasoning or literature support.

HD.descriptor: Clearly articulated multi-point feedback plan specifying: timing of oral/written feedback relative to student work; form of feedback (written annotations, verbal conferencing, peer feedback, self-assessment); how feedback language will be differentiated for Aboriginal students and EAL/D learners; how feedback will inform subsequent teaching. Explicitly connected to syllabus outcomes. Demonstrates research-informed feedback principles.

D.descriptor: Well-developed feedback plan with clear timing, form, and connection to outcomes. Differentiation for the cohort addressed. Feed-forward orientation evident.

C.descriptor: Feedback plan with appropriate timing and form. Some differentiation for the cohort. Connection to outcomes evident though may be general.

P.descriptor: Feedback plan present but timing or form may be vague. Limited differentiation. Connection to outcomes asserted rather than demonstrated.

N.descriptor: Feedback planning absent, superficial, or not connected to outcomes or the cohort.

HD.descriptor: Deep, critical reflection. Clearly identifies specific pedagogical insights and explains how thinking has changed. Explicitly links reflection to future teaching practice. Analytical rather than descriptive; may draw on relevant literature.

D.descriptor: Strong reflective thinking. Clearly identifies learning gained and implications for future teaching. Moves beyond description into analysis.

C.descriptor: Reflective thinking with clear identification of learning gained. Some analytical elements, though may be partly descriptive.

P.descriptor: Largely descriptive. Identifies what was done but limited insight into learning or future improvement.

N.descriptor: Superficial, minimal, or not submitted. No clear evidence of reflective thinking.

assessmentTasks.hdCallout: HD requires synthesis across parts — your Part C justification must explicitly connect back to specific decisions visible in Part B, and your D1 feedback plan must name cohort-specific differentiation strategies, not generic feedback principles.
checklist: Part A task outline complete with correct NSW syllabus outcomes
Forum post submitted by 8 March (100 words, names discipline, topic, module, group/individual choice, first-hand experience)
Five-week program on supplied template (one A4 page per week)
10 hours of model construction/portfolio time integrated across the program
At least two formative assessment tasks per week
Differentiation specific to named cohort (Aboriginal students, EAL/D learners)
Part C essay cites peer-reviewed literature
D1 feedback plan specifies timing, form, language differentiation, and feed-forward
D2 reflection is analytical, not purely descriptive
Submitted as single PDF on the template by 5 April
submissionInstructions: Save as PDF and submit as one document
Reference list and syllabus extracts are not included in the word count
Word count must be within ±10% of the required word count

assessmentTasks.title: Assessment Design — Critique, Redesign, and Reflection
assessmentTasks.rationale: Teachers must be able to critique, adapt and redesign assessment tasks to ensure they are valid, fair, syllabus-aligned, and responsive to the specific cohort they teach.
assessmentTasks.aim: Demonstrate knowledge of sound assessment principles including internal alignment, syllabus alignment, and differentiation to support diverse learners while maintaining intellectual rigour.

parts.title: Critique of an existing assessment task
parts.description: Academic essay critiquing a provided Stage 6 science assessment task for suitability for the given cohort.
requirements: Evaluate assessment design quality using validity, reliability, fairness, accessibility, and internal alignment
Demonstrate NSW Stage 6 syllabus alignment including outcomes, content, and Working Scientifically requirements
Analyse cognitive demand and scientific thinking required (reasoning, conceptual demand, depth of explanation)
Address cohort responsiveness and equity including differentiation and cultural responsiveness for Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander students
Consider safety and feasibility where practical work is involved
Use peer-reviewed literature to support claims
parts.note: Critique the task as an assessment instrument — how well it elicits valid evidence of learning — not only whether it is engaging.

parts.title: Redesigned assessment task
parts.description: Rewrite the Part A assessment task incorporating improvements identified and justified in the critique.
requirements: Explicit alignment to NSW Stage 6 syllabus (outcomes, content, Working Scientifically)
Internal alignment: student instructions ↔ what is assessed ↔ criteria ↔ evidence produced
Differentiation appropriate to cohort without reducing rigour
At least one purposeful feedback point (teacher, peer, or self-assessment) before final submission
Meaningful ICT integration where appropriate — not simply typed submission
guidanceNotes: Recommended headings: Task title + course + year / NSW syllabus outcomes / Task description and purpose / Student instructions including conditions / Evidence to be submitted / Marking criteria / Differentiation, supports and extensions / Feedback checkpoint(s) / Safety and ethical considerations

parts.title: Reflection
parts.description: Reflection on learning in this task.
requirements: Where you were most engaged or challenged
One significant insight about assessment design in Stage 6 Science
How you will apply this process in future teaching

HD.descriptor: Clear, detailed and well-organised critique. Accurately explains and applies validity, fairness, internal alignment and cognitive demand. Suitability for the cohort clearly analysed.

D.descriptor: Strong critique applying assessment concepts accurately. Explains suitability for cohort.

C.descriptor: Identifies strengths and weaknesses with some use of assessment concepts. Cohort discussion uneven.

P.descriptor: Mostly descriptive. Limited evaluation using assessment concepts. Cohort link general.

N.descriptor: Very limited or inaccurate critique. Little understanding of assessment principles.

HD.descriptor: Accurately identifies relevant Stage 6 outcomes and content. Clearly analyses Working Scientifically skills, scientific reasoning and safety where relevant.

D.descriptor: Correctly identifies outcomes and content. Clear explanation including Working Scientifically elements.

C.descriptor: Appropriate outcomes identified but alignment explanation lacks depth. Working Scientifically mentioned but limited analysis.

P.descriptor: Limited or unclear reference to outcomes. Alignment mostly asserted. Minimal Working Scientifically reference.

N.descriptor: Syllabus incorrectly referenced or not referenced.

rubric.criterion: Use of assessment theory and literature

HD.descriptor: Relevant peer-reviewed literature strengthens and justifies evaluation. Sources clearly connected to argument.

D.descriptor: Relevant literature supports key claims clearly.

C.descriptor: Literature used appropriately but links to critique sometimes underdeveloped.

P.descriptor: Limited or uneven use of literature.

N.descriptor: Little or no appropriate peer-reviewed literature.

HD.descriptor: Clear, professional task. Explicit alignment between outcomes, instructions, evidence and criteria. Feedback checkpoint built into process. ICT meaningfully enhances learning. Changes address Part A critique.

D.descriptor: Clear and well-aligned task. Feedback and ICT used appropriately. Most issues from Part A addressed.

C.descriptor: Workable task with general alignment. Feedback and ICT included but not fully developed.

P.descriptor: Alignment inconsistent or unclear. Feedback or ICT appear added without clear purpose.

N.descriptor: Unclear, incomplete or poorly aligned task.

HD.descriptor: Adjustments clearly support diverse learners including Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander students. Access increased without lowering expectations.

D.descriptor: Appropriate adjustments for a range of learners. Cohort diversity clearly considered.

C.descriptor: Some adjustments included though may be general or uneven.

P.descriptor: Limited or unclear adjustments. Cohort mentioned but not embedded in design.

N.descriptor: Little or no consideration of cohort diversity.

HD.descriptor: Thoughtful reflection showing clear learning and future classroom application.

D.descriptor: Clear reflection with practical connections to teaching.

assessmentTasks.hdCallout: HD in Part B requires your redesigned task to explicitly address every gap you identified in Part A — markers will check for direct correspondence between your critique and your redesign decisions.
checklist: Part A written as an academic essay (not a checklist or bullet-point critique)
Assessment concepts applied by name: validity, reliability, fairness, internal alignment, cognitive demand
NSW Stage 6 syllabus outcomes and Working Scientifically requirements explicitly referenced
Cohort responsiveness addressed including Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander students
Peer-reviewed literature integrated throughout Part A
Part B redesign addresses every gap identified in Part A
Internal alignment explicit: instructions ↔ criteria ↔ evidence
At least one feedback checkpoint included in Part B
ICT integration meaningful, not cosmetic
Differentiation embedded in Part B design, not added as an afterthought
Submitted as one PDF by 10 May
submissionInstructions: Reference list and syllabus extracts are not included in the word count

supportCallout: Anything going on that may impact your success in this unit? Don't hesitate to reach out to your lecturer sooner rather than later. We're here to support you.

announcementBody.intro: Welcome to EDSE358. This unit develops your capacity to plan, assess, and report in senior science — the three responsibilities that sit at the centre of a science teacher's professional work. Over the trimester you will move from reading the syllabus as a governing document to designing and justifying your own teaching, assessment, and feedback decisions against it.
announcementBody.focus: Before Module 1 begins next week, take time to explore how the unit fits together. Open the Unit Outline and read the assessment overview, find the Learning Materials and Assessment Portal in the navigation block, and note the day and time of your weekly live session. Check the two assessment tasks and their due dates now — both shape how you should pace your work from the first week.

1.title: The Senior Science Syllabus
notes: Make a plan to complete them 🗓️

announcementBody.intro: Welcome to EDSE358. This week we begin with Module 1 — an introduction to the senior science syllabus as the governing framework for everything you will plan, assess, and report against as a science teacher.
announcementBody.focus: Module 1 establishes the senior science syllabus as the authoritative document shaping your teaching practice. You will examine the structure of the syllabus for your teaching area and begin to understand how outcomes, content, and assessment requirements connect.

1.liveSessionFocus: In our first live session, we set the scene for the unit, clarify expectations around assessment, and begin exploring the syllabus structures that underpin your work as a senior science teacher.
liveSessionTasks: Download both assessment tasks from the Assessment Portal and review the requirements before the session.
Choose your primary teaching area and review its senior science syllabus — note the key components: outcomes, content, and assessment requirements.
Come ready to share one thing that surprised you about the syllabus structure and one question you would like to discuss.

links.recording: <iframe allowfullscreen="" frameborder="0" src="https://echo360.net.au/public/media/6228228f-fac3-415e-a8cf-32a18f1c4f7c?autoplay=false&automute=false" style="position:absolute; top:0; left:0; width:100%; height:100%;">                     </iframe>

forumPrompts: Think about an assessment task you've seen, done, or been given on placement. Was it clearly anchored to syllabus outcomes? Or did it feel like it was assessing something else — effort, neatness, general science knowledge? One example, one honest observation. No essays needed.

sections.heading: Key Outcomes
items: This session introduced EDSE358's two assessment tasks and clarified expectations for both.
Students received guidance on unit program scope, word count, syllabus selection, and the concept of scientific modelling.
Key questions from students shaped a practical understanding of what each task requires.

sections.heading: Decisions Made
items: AT1: Use the 2017 NSW syllabus — not the 2025 version, despite the upcoming transition.
AT1 scope: Covers 5 weeks of a teaching and learning program (part-module, not a full module).
AT1 weighting: 60%; AT2 weighting: 40%.
Formative assessment: Minimum two pieces per week required = 8–10 total across the program.
Model complexity: A model of an atom alone is likely too simple for Stage 6; models must reflect deeper content (e.g., SPDF notation, electron configuration).
Teaching area: Students should design AT1 around their primary science subject (e.g., biology graduates teach Biology Stage 6 only; cross-subject flexibility applies only to Years 7–10).

sections.heading: Assignment Clarifications
items: AT1 structure: Backwards-design a 5-week learning program leading to a student model-construction task; include learning activities, outcomes, and formative assessment.
Unit plan vs. lesson plan: Unit plans are week-level overviews — not minute-by-minute; aim for the midpoint between a year plan and a lesson plan.
Word count — "2,500 words equivalent": Excludes syllabus codes and content descriptors; budget ~500 words per week across 5 weeks.
Going over word count: Stephen will request trimming; efficiency of expression is an assessed skill.
AT2: Critique a Queensland assessment task, identify misalignment to NSW syllabus, then redesign the task and marking key.

Portfolio (AT1): A collection of student work artifacts across the 5 weeks — can include worksheets, design templates, experiment photos, or structured classwork; not limited to model construction only.
Model construction time: Up to 10 hours; recommended to stagger and ramp up across weeks rather than concentrating at the end.

sections.heading: What Is a "Model" in Science
items: A model is an abstraction — strips away detail to highlight key components and their relationships.
Examples: Bohr atom, SPDF electron configuration, coal seam software models, weather charts, collision theory, acid-base models (Lewis, Arrhenius).
Students should also understand limitations of models, not just construct them.
The quality of AT1 is judged by how well the learning program prepares students to build a meaningful, conceptually deep model.

sections.heading: Pending Confirmation
items: Detailed unpacking videos for AT1 and AT2 still to be published.
Stephen to source and post a good example of a teaching and learning program at the appropriate scale.
Stephen to follow up with Sue regarding her draft feedback request.
Whether AITSL standards will be required in the unit program template — Stephen flagged this as under review.

sections.heading: Action Items
items: Stephen: Post example teaching and learning program alongside session summary.
Stephen: Publish detailed unpacking videos for AT1 and AT2.
Stephen: Follow up with Sue on draft review request.
Students: Begin AT1 after completing Module 3 / 3B.
Students: Download the Word template to understand expected detail level per week.
Students (optional): Submit a draft of Week 1 for early directional feedback — Stephen will review partial drafts, not full submissions.
Extensions & Logistics: Flexible portal opens one week before the AT1 due date; grants an additional 7 days.
Use the Assignment 1 and Assignment 2 forums for questions between sessions — Stephen will respond and address in live sessions.
Constructive alignment map available in the study schedule showing how modules, activities, and assessments align.

2.title: The Nature of Science Teaching

announcementBody.intro: This week we explore the nature of science as a discipline and what it means to teach it well in a senior secondary context. Module 2 asks you to think carefully about the kind of science teacher you want to be.
announcementBody.focus: Module 2 focuses on the Nature of Science (NOS) — the values, practices, and epistemological commitments that characterise science as a way of knowing. Understanding NOS shapes how you select content, design experiences, and communicate science to students.

2.liveSessionFocus: Session 2 covers Modules 1 and 2 together. We will examine the syllabus as a professional document and explore how an understanding of NOS can shape your approach to planning senior science lessons.
liveSessionTasks: Review the lecture recordings and readings for both Modules 1 and 2 before the session.
Identify one aspect of the Nature of Science that you find most difficult to communicate to senior students.
Consider: how does your teaching area's syllabus reflect or constrain a NOS-informed approach to teaching?
forumPrompts: What does it mean to teach in the 21st century? Cite evidence to support your response.
What does the science teacher know? Cite evidence to support your response.
What does the science teacher do? Cite evidence to support your response.

sections.heading: Summary
items: The session explored how the nature of science (NOS) is defined, embedded in NSW syllabus documents, and its implications for science teaching.
Participants identified that NOS is not explicitly stated in syllabuses but is embedded through Working Scientifically skills and inquiry language.
The discussion connected NOS tenets to assessment design, emphasising that assessments should reflect science as a process, not fact recall.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Working Scientifically is the primary vehicle through which NOS is represented in Stage 6 syllabus documents, accounting for 60% of assessment weighting.
Assessments must align to syllabus outcomes, not historical trivia (e.g., dates or biographical facts about Darwin).
The 100-word forum post counts as a submission component; it should also be copy-pasted into the final assessment task and may be revised before final submission.
A 5-week sequence is the target scope for Assessment Task 1 — not a full module; prior knowledge context must be stated clearly.

sections.heading: Key Concepts Discussed
items: NOS tenets identified by participants: tentative, evidence-based, dynamic, collaborative, creative, self-correcting, and socially/culturally influenced.
NOS is embedded in syllabus language via cognitive verbs (investigate, analyse, explain) and inquiry questions that drive learning sequences.
Historical science examples (Darwin/Wallace, Louis Pasteur, Robert Koch) serve as classroom anchors for demonstrating NOS in practice — how theories are built, tested, and refined over time.
Bruner's spiral curriculum cited as the pedagogical rationale for revisiting concepts across Stage 3–6 with increasing complexity.

sections.heading: Assessment Task 1 Clarifications
items: Model definition: Must be something students can interact with and change — not a static poster; it should represent the behaviour of a complex system.

Acceptable model examples: choose-your-own-adventure disease transmission activity, Glitterbug powder transmission simulation with mathematical modelling of R-values, fluid mosaic model, or a game representing infectious disease spread.
A gallery walk poster alone does not qualify as a model; interactivity is required.
Scope: Select a chunk of a module (e.g., first or last 5 weeks); clearly state prior knowledge before sequencing begins.

sections.heading: Forum Review
items: Participants to review forum Q&A threads where Stephen will post a digest of assessment-related questions by end of week.

sections.heading: Action Items
items: All students: Review NSW NESA Stage 6 syllabus documents (2017); locate Working Scientifically sections and identify embedded NOS language.
All students: Finalise forum post (100-word summary) and incorporate into Assessment Task 1 draft; revise as needed before final submission.
Stephen: Compile forum question digest and post by end of week.
Next session: Module 3 — Planning and programming, directly aligned to Assessment Task 1 (learning sequence design using backwards by design / Wiggins & McTighe).

3.title: Planning and Assessing Learning Models and Constructivist Teaching

announcementBody.intro: This week we begin Module 3, turning our attention to how students learn and what that means for how we plan and teach. Understanding learning models — and in particular constructivist approaches — is foundational to everything that follows in this unit, including how we design assessment that genuinely supports learning.
announcementBody.focus: Module 3A focuses on the theoretical underpinnings of effective teaching and learning in senior science. As you work through the materials, keep asking yourself: how does this change the way I would plan a lesson or a unit of work?
announcementBody.keyIdea: A key idea this week is that learning is not passive reception — it is active construction. Your job as a teacher is to design experiences that give students something worth constructing.

3.liveSessionFocus: Consolidate learning about learning models, constructivist teaching, and their relationship to planning learning and designing assessment.
liveSessionTasks: Write 3–5 key takeaways.
Note one question you want to test or discuss.
Share one key idea you would apply in your own planning.
Respond to a peer in the forum.
3.orientationNote: Module 3A establishes the theoretical foundations that will underpin everything in Modules 4A through 4D — and in Assessment Task 1. The constructivist models you study this week (5Es, IGASAR, and Cognitive Load Theory) are not background reading: they are the conceptual framework you will draw on to justify your pedagogical decisions in AT1 Part C1. At the same time, Part A of AT1 — your task outline and 100-word forum post — is due this week. Your forum post should already show constructivist thinking in action: the choices you make about your discipline, topic, and individual-versus-group structure should reflect the principles you are studying now, not just what feels familiar. Engage with Module 3A materials before you post.
forumPrompts: Choose either the 5Es or IGASAR model and apply it to plan a brief learning sequence (three to five steps) for a specific topic in your Stage 6 science discipline. Briefly describe what students would do at each phase. Then identify: which phase of the model creates the greatest challenge for a beginning teacher, and why? Post your response to the Module 3A forum.

Looking ahead to Assessment Task 1 Part A: you will need to justify your choice of assessment as learning task using constructivist principles. Identify one aspect of the constructivist approach you studied this week that you remain uncertain about. How might that uncertainty affect the quality of your reasoning in Part A? What would help you resolve it before the submission deadline?
3.workedExample:
WORKED EXAMPLE 1 — Biology, 5Es model

Topic: Enzymes and catalase activity (Biology Module 1: Cells as the Basis of Life — cell requirements)

ENGAGE — Demonstrate fresh liver dropped into hydrogen peroxide. The vigorous fizzing prompts the question "what's in living tissue that does this?" and surfaces students' prior ideas about what cells contain.

EXPLORE — In pairs, students investigate how catalase activity changes across a temperature range (e.g. boiled vs. chilled vs. room-temperature liver), recording observations before any formal explanation is given.

EXPLAIN — Building on what students observed, introduce enzyme structure, the active site, and denaturation — formalising the language to account for the pattern in their own data.

ELABORATE — Students apply the concept to a new context (e.g. why a fever is dangerous, or why detergents use enzymes that tolerate heat).

Greatest challenge — The Explain phase. The temptation for a beginning teacher is to front-load the enzyme theory before students have explored, which collapses the sequence back into transmission teaching. The skill is holding back, judging when students are ready, and constructing the explanation from their observations rather than overriding them — a genuine test of pacing and questioning.

WORKED EXAMPLE 2 — Chemistry, IGASAR model

Topic: The reactivity series of metals (Chemistry Module 3: Reactive Chemistry — comparative reactivity)

INITIATE — Pose the question "Can we predict how vigorously a metal will react?" and have students reflect on prior ideas (e.g. why some metals corrode and others don't).

GATHER INFO — Students react a range of metals (Mg, Zn, Fe, Cu) with dilute acid, recording rate, gas evolution, and temperature change.

ANALYSE — Students compare and rank their observations, categorising metals by relative vigour.

SYNTHESISE & CONCLUDE — From the ranked data, students construct a reactivity order and generalise the pattern.

APPLY — Students predict the outcome of a displacement reaction (e.g. zinc in copper sulfate) using the series they built.

Greatest challenge — The Gather info phase. Open practical inquiry asks a beginning teacher to manage safety, behaviour, and time simultaneously, while also ensuring the data students collect is consistent enough to support a valid conclusion. Messy or non-comparable observations here will undermine every later phase — so scaffolding the method without simply prescribing it is the hard balance.

4.title: Planning and Assessing Programs and Lesson Plans

announcementBody.intro: This week we move from theory to practice, applying the learning models and constructivist principles from Module 3A to the design of programs and lesson plans. Good planning is not about filling time — it is about making deliberate decisions that connect learning intentions, teaching strategies, and assessment evidence.
announcementBody.focus: Module 3B focuses on how experienced teachers translate curriculum and pedagogy into coherent, purposeful plans. As you work through the materials, pay attention to the decisions being made and why — not just the format of the plan itself.
announcementBody.keyIdea: A key idea this week is that planning is a professional act. The choices you make about sequence, timing, and strategy reflect your understanding of your students, your subject, and your purpose as a teacher.

4.orientationNote: Module 3B builds directly on the constructivist and learning model foundations from Module 3A. As you work through programs and lesson plans this week, pay attention to two things that connect directly to Assessment Task 1: how ICT can be used purposefully to enhance learning (not just support it), and how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives can be embedded authentically into science content from the outset of planning — not added on afterwards. Both will be assessed in AT1 Part B. Safety is a pedagogical design consideration — not just an administrative requirement. As you plan your practical activities, ask: how does your choice of activity affect the safety demands on students and on you as a teacher? How does this influence your planning decisions?
4.liveSessionFocus: Apply learning models and constructivist planning principles to program and lesson planning.
liveSessionTasks: Work through the Module 3B book.
Identify your 'must-do' planning moves.
Note one challenge you want help with.
Bring planning questions to the live session.
Comment on a program/plan idea from the module.
Respond to at least one peer.
forumPrompts: Review two digital tools relevant to your science discipline (e.g. PhET simulations, Geogebra, NESA data sets, or equivalent). For each tool, explain how it enhances — not merely supports — a specific syllabus outcome. What does the tool make possible that a non-digital approach could not?

Identify one opportunity to embed an Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspective into a topic in your chosen science discipline. Explain its connection to a specific syllabus outcome and how you would frame it for students as integral to the science, not as an add-on.
List the activity types used across the week you selected from the sample unit. Does the unit use a sufficient range? What types are missing and why might that matter for student learning?

5.title: Assessing and Reporting — Assessment for learning

announcementBody.intro: This week we begin Module 4, which runs across four parts and sits at the heart of this unit. We start with the most important question: why do we assess? Before designing any assessment task or rubric, it is worth understanding what assessment is actually for — and how it can be used to move learning forward rather than simply measure it.
announcementBody.focus: Module 4A focuses on assessment for learning — the use of feedback, questioning, and peer and self-reflection to improve student understanding during the learning process. As you work through the materials, think about how assessment can be woven into teaching rather than added on at the end.
announcementBody.keyIdea: A key idea this week is that the most powerful assessment happens before the final mark — in the moments where a teacher notices where a student is and responds.

5.orientationNote: Modules 4A through 4D form a deliberate spiral. Module 4A establishes why we assess and how feedback moves learning forward. Module 4B applies that thinking to designing assessment instruments. Module 4C extends this to differentiated and inclusive assessment. Module 4D brings it together through rubric design and feedback practice. Assessment Task 1 draws on all four modules — and Assessment Task 2 asks you to apply everything you have learned across the sequence. As you work through each module, keep asking: how does this change the way I would design or respond to an assessment task?
5.liveSessionFocus: Identify where teacher feedback, peer input, and student self-reflection can move learning forward in a portfolio task.
liveSessionTasks: Work through the Module 4A learning materials.
Watch the lecture.
Write 3–5 key takeaways.
Note one question to bring forward.
Bring one example of where feedback, peer input, or self-reflection could move learning forward in the portfolio task.
Post feedback and questioning ideas to the 4A forum.
forumPrompts: Using the portfolio example, decide where there might be natural points for feedback from the teacher and from peers. Locate or suggest places where student self-reflection would be appropriate.
At the teacher feedback points, develop a set of questions that teachers might use to elicit student thinking. Post these to the 4A forum for discussion.

6.title: Assessing and Reporting — Designing assessment instruments

assessments.name: Assessment Task 1 — Parts B, C & D (55%)

announcementBody.intro: This week we move from the purpose of assessment to its design. Designing a good assessment instrument is a complex professional task — it requires you to be clear about what you are assessing, how students will demonstrate it, and how you will know quality when you see it.
announcementBody.focus: Module 4B focuses on constructive alignment: the relationship between syllabus outcomes, task design, and marking criteria. A well-designed task makes this alignment visible and gives students a genuine opportunity to demonstrate their learning.
announcementBody.keyIdea: A key idea this week is that assessment design is curriculum design. The choices you make about task type, conditions, and criteria shape what students learn and how they think about their own progress.

6.liveSessionFocus: Explore alignment between syllabus outcomes, task design, and marking criteria.
liveSessionTasks: Work through the Module 4B materials.
Complete the syllabus mapping exercise.
Watch the two Module 4B lecture recordings.
Write 3–5 key takeaways.
Note one question to bring forward.
Bring one question about alignment between syllabus outcomes, task design, and marking criteria.
Contribute a short post about the syllabus mapping exercise conducted on a task and marking rubric.
6.orientationNote: Module 4B sits at the heart of Assessment Task 1. The constructive alignment principles you study this week — the relationship between outcomes, task design, the evidence students produce, and marking criteria — are the same principles you need to demonstrate in your AT1 unit program (Part B) and justify in your essays (Part C1). Pay particular attention to the evaluation strategy requirement in Part B4: the rubric asks whether your sequence of formative and summative tasks tells a coherent story about student learning across the five weeks. Work through Module 4B with your unit program in mind — every alignment principle you internalise this week will make your Part B design stronger and your Part C1 justification more precise.

additionalLectures.label: Module 4B Assessment Task Design for Stage 6

forumPrompts: Find an assessment task used in a NSW Stage 6 science classroom. Analyse its alignment: do the outcomes, instructions, evidence students produce, and marking criteria all point to the same thing? Identify one strength and one gap. Then identify or suggest a feedback mechanism — how would a teacher respond to student work on this task in a way that moves learning forward?

Looking at the sample unit as a whole, what is its evaluation strategy? How do the formative and summative tasks together tell the teacher whether students have achieved the targeted outcomes? Is this strategy coherent and sufficient?
6.workedExample:
STRONG ALIGNMENT — Stage 6 Chemistry, Module 5: Equilibrium and Acid Reactions

Outcome: CH12-6 — explains the relationship between the rate of a chemical reaction and the frequency of effective collisions.

Task instruction: Design and conduct an investigation to determine how temperature affects the rate of the reaction between sodium thiosulfate and hydrochloric acid. Record your data, identify a trend, and explain it using collision theory.

Evidence produced: A data table, a graph showing rate vs temperature, and a written explanation using collision theory.

Marking criterion: Accurately explains the relationship between temperature, particle energy, collision frequency, and reaction rate using collected data.

Why this is strong: The outcome, instruction, evidence, and criterion all require the same thinking — applying collision theory to data. A student who understands the outcome knows exactly what to do and why.

PARTIAL ALIGNMENT — Stage 6 Biology, Module 5: Heredity

Outcome: BIO12-12 — analyses the relationship between inheritance patterns and molecular genetics.

Task instruction: Research Mendel's experiments and write a 500-word report on his findings.

Evidence produced: A descriptive written report.

Marking criterion: Report is 400–600 words, uses correct terminology, and includes a bibliography.

Why this is partial: The outcome requires analysis of the relationship between inheritance patterns and molecular genetics. The task produces a descriptive report about historical experiments. The marking criterion rewards length and terminology — not analytical thinking. A student could score full marks without demonstrating the outcome.

7.title: Assessing and Reporting — Designing differentiated assessment

announcementBody.intro: This week we examine one of the most demanding aspects of assessment design: creating tasks that are accessible to all students without compromising the rigour of the syllabus outcomes. In a senior science classroom, your students will bring a wide range of learning needs, cultural backgrounds, and prior experiences — and your assessment design needs to account for this from the outset.
announcementBody.focus: Module 4C focuses on inclusive assessment design — adjustments, UDL principles, and the NESA and NSW DoE frameworks that support diverse learners. As you work through the materials, pay attention to the distinction between adjustments that change how a student demonstrates learning and changes that alter what is being assessed.
announcementBody.keyIdea: A key idea this week is that inclusion in assessment is not about lowering the bar — it is about ensuring every student can reach it.

7.liveSessionFocus: Consider how assessment tasks can be adjusted for inclusion without changing the intended syllabus outcome.
liveSessionTasks: Work through Module 4C materials with an eye on inclusion in assessment design.
Watch the NSW DoE website resource guide.
Watch the Module 4C differentiated assessment lecture.
Write 3–5 key takeaways.
Note one question to bring forward.
Bring one example of how an assessment task could be adjusted for inclusion without changing the outcome.
Reflect on how inclusion is, or is not, evident in an assessment task notification.
7.orientationNote: Module 4C prepares you directly for two of the most closely assessed requirements in Assessment Task 1. The differentiation you embed in your unit program (Part B) needs to be specific — not 'I would differentiate for diverse learners' but 'I would adjust X strategy because students with Y characteristic need Z support to access the outcome'. The resource justification essay in Part C2 should also explain why your chosen resources suit the specific needs of your named cohort. Students who engage seriously with the UDL principles and adjustment frameworks in Module 4C will find that their AT1 unit program becomes more precise and their Part C2 reasoning more grounded in evidence. The differentiation you specify in Part B is what the Part C2 essay should justify.
forumPrompts: Select one differentiation strategy from the Module 4C materials — a UDL principle, an access arrangement, or a specific adjustment approach — and explain how you would embed it in one week of your AT1 unit program. Be specific: name the cohort characteristic you are responding to, describe what you would adjust in your program, and explain how the adjustment maintains access to the syllabus outcome without changing what is being assessed. Post your response to the Module 4C forum.

Open your AT1 unit program draft (or sketch a draft if you have not yet started). Find one place where differentiation is explicitly stated and one place where it is present but implied — described in general terms rather than specified for your cohort. For the implied differentiation: what specific language or detail would you need to add to make it explicit enough to satisfy the Part B marking rubric? Post your findings to the forum.

8.title: Assessing and Reporting — Developing rubrics and providing feedback

announcementBody.intro: This week we bring Module 4 to a close by focusing on how teachers make quality visible through rubrics and feedback. These are central tools for supporting student learning, guiding improvement, and ensuring that assessment genuinely informs teaching.
announcementBody.focus: Module 4D focuses on how teachers design and use effective rubrics and provide meaningful feedback that moves learning forward.
announcementBody.keyIdea: A key idea in this module is that rubrics are not simply marking tools — they are learning tools. Similarly, feedback is most powerful when it helps students understand where they are in their learning, where they need to go next, and how to get there.

8.liveSessionFocus: Explore what makes a rubric useful and how feedback can be designed to improve learning.
liveSessionTasks: Work through the Module 4D materials in Moodle.
Watch the lecture on rubrics and feedback.
Prepare for the live session and note down examples or questions.
Bring one question about what makes a rubric useful or what makes feedback likely to improve learning.
Post a short reflection to the Module 4D forum.
Work through the Module 4D materials on rubrics and feedback.
Read the anonymised sample student response provided in the module.

Write 100–150 words of feedback for this student. Your feedback must: (a) identify one specific strength linked to a criterion, (b) provide one actionable improvement with explicit guidance on how to address it, and (c) include one forward-looking comment connecting to the next task.
Post your feedback to the Module 4D forum and respond to one peer's post, noting one thing they did well in their feedback.
8.orientationNote: Module 4D brings the teaching program to a close and focuses directly on what is now an assessed criterion in Assessment Task 1: feedback planning. AT1 Part D1 (5 marks) asks you to articulate a specific feedback plan for your unit — specifying when feedback will occur, what form it will take, how feedback language will be differentiated for your named cohort (including Aboriginal students and EAL/D learners), and how feedback will inform your subsequent teaching decisions. The forum activity this week — writing 100 to 150 words of feedback for an anonymised student response — is a direct rehearsal for Part D1 thinking. Approach it seriously. AT1 submission closes 5 April.
workflow: Work through the Module 4D materials in Moodle
Watch the lecture on rubrics and feedback
Prepare for the live session and note down examples or questions
Join the live session and contribute your thinking
forumPrompts: What makes a rubric useful for both teachers and students?
How can feedback be designed to promote improvement rather than just justify a mark?
What challenges do you anticipate in providing effective feedback in senior science?
